# Get the review data and user data from previous queries
users_2016 = var_functions_query_db_8
reviews_by_2016_users = var_functions_query_db_10

# Extract business_refs from the reviews
business_refs = [review['business_ref'] for review in reviews_by_2016_users]
unique_business_refs = list(set(business_refs))

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_reviews': len(reviews_by_2016_users),
    'unique_businesses': len(unique_business_refs),
    'sample_business_refs': unique_business_refs[:10]
}))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
# Read the full business data from file
import json

# The result is stored as a file path
business_file_path = "data/query_db/result_e7b8a8a5c9f1d2e3b4f5a6c7d8e9f0a1.json"

with open(business_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Now get reviews by 2016 users
reviews_file_path = "data/query_db/result_10.json"
with open(reviews_file_path, 'r') as f:
    reviews = json.load(f)

# Get all businesses that were reviewed by 2016 users
business_refs_in_reviews = set(review['business_ref'] for review in reviews)

# Convert business_ref to business_id format (businessref_X -> businessid_X)
business_ids_in_reviews = set()
for ref in business_refs_in_reviews:
    if ref.startswith('businessref_'):
        business_id = 'businessid_' + ref.split('_')[1]
        business_ids_in_reviews.add(business_id)

# Filter businesses
reviewed_businesses = [b for b in businesses if b['business_id'] in business_ids_in_reviews]

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_businesses': len(businesses),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'unique_businesses_reviewed': len(reviewed_businesses)
}))

result.preview:
"DuckDB query execution error (CatalogException): Catalog Error: Table with name business does not exist!
Did you mean "yelp_user.user"?

LINE 9:   FROM businessinfo_database.business
               ^"

args.query:
SELECT r.review_id, r.user_id, r.business_ref, r.date, b.description 
FROM review r 
JOIN (
  SELECT user_id FROM user 
  WHERE yelping_since >= '2016-01-01' AND yelping_since < '2017-01-01'
) u ON r.user_id = u.user_id 
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT business_id, description 
  FROM businessinfo_database.business
) b ON r.business_ref = 'businessref_' || substr(b.business_id, 11)
LIMIT 100

exec_args.sql:
SELECT r.review_id, r.user_id, r.business_ref, r.date, b.description 
FROM review r 
JOIN (
  SELECT user_id FROM user 
  WHERE yelping_since >= '2016-01-01' AND yelping_since < '2017-01-01'
) u ON r.user_id = u.user_id 
LEFT JOIN (
  SELECT business_id, description 
  FROM businessinfo_database.business
) b ON r.business_ref = 'businessref_' || substr(b.business_id, 11)
LIMIT 100

args.code:
import json
from collections import defaultdict
import re

# Get the business data and review data
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:38']
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create a mapping from business_id to business info
# business_id format: businessid_X
# business_ref format: businessref_X
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    # Extract the number part
    if '_' in business_id:
        business_num = business_id.split('_')[1]
        # Also store by number for easy lookup
        business_map[business_num] = business

# Parse categories from business descriptions
def extract_categories(description):
    """Extract categories from business description."""
    if not description or 'services in' not in description:
        return []
    
    # Find the part after "services in" or "including" or similar
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|offers a range of services in|including|categories of|in the categories of)\s+([^.]+)',
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and "and"
            categories = re.split(r',|\sand\s', categories_text)
            # Clean up each category
            categories = [cat.strip().rstrip('.') for cat in categories]
            categories = [cat for cat in categories if cat]
            return categories
    
    return []

# Count reviews per category
category_review_count = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        if business_num in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_num]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            for category in categories:
                category_review_count[category] += 1

# Sort by count and get top 5
sorted_categories = sorted(category_review_count.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
top_5 = sorted_categories[:5]

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'review_count': len(reviews),
    'businesses_with_categories': len(business_map),
    'category_counts': dict(category_review_count),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}))

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import defaultdict
import re

# Get the business data and review data
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:38']
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create a mapping from business_id to business info
# business_id format: businessid_X
# business_ref format: businessref_X
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    # Extract the number part
    if '_' in business_id:
        business_num = business_id.split('_')[1]
        # Also store by number for easy lookup
        business_map[business_num] = business

# Parse categories from business descriptions
def extract_categories(description):
    """Extract categories from business description."""
    if not description or 'services in' not in description:
        return []
    
    # Find the part after "services in" or "including" or similar
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|offers a range of services in|including|categories of|in the categories of)\s+([^.]+)',
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, description, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            categories_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and "and"
            categories = re.split(r',|\sand\s', categories_text)
            # Clean up each category
            categories = [cat.strip().rstrip('.') for cat in categories]
            categories = [cat for cat in categories if cat]
            return categories
    
    return []

# Count reviews per category
category_review_count = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        if business_num in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_num]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            for category in categories:
                category_review_count[category] += 1

# Sort by count and get top 5
sorted_categories = sorted(category_review_count.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
top_5 = sorted_categories[:5]

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'review_count': len(reviews),
    'businesses_with_categories': len(business_map),
    'category_counts': dict(category_review_count),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
from collections import defaultdict
import re

# Get the data - both are lists
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:38']
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create mapping from business number to business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    if '_' in business_id:
        business_num = business_id.split('_')[1]
        business_map[business_num] = business

# Parse categories from description
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description or 'services in' not in description:
        return []
    
    # Find categories after patterns like "services in", "including", etc.
    description_lower = description.lower()
    
    if 'services in' in description_lower:
        start = description_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        # Get the text after this phrase, up to the end or a period
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        # Split by commas and "and"
        parts = re.split(r',| and ', remaining)
        categories = [part.strip() for part in parts if part.strip()]
        return categories
    
    return []

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        if business_num in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_num]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1

# Sort and get top 5
sorted_categories = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
top_5 = sorted_categories[:5]

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'businesses_mapped': len(business_map),
    'categories_found': len(category_counts),
    'top_5': top_5
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import defaultdict
import re

# Get the data - both are lists
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:38']
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create mapping from business number to business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    if '_' in business_id:
        business_num = business_id.split('_')[1]
        business_map[business_num] = business

# Parse categories from description
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description or 'services in' not in description:
        return []
    
    # Find categories after patterns like "services in", "including", etc.
    description_lower = description.lower()
    
    if 'services in' in description_lower:
        start = description_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        # Get the text after this phrase, up to the end or a period
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        # Split by commas and "and"
        parts = re.split(r',| and ', remaining)
        categories = [part.strip() for part in parts if part.strip()]
        return categories
    
    return []

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        if business_num in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_num]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1

# Sort and get top 5
sorted_categories = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
top_5 = sorted_categories[:5]

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'businesses_mapped': len(business_map),
    'categories_found': len(category_counts),
    'top_5': top_5
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
from collections import defaultdict, Counter
import re

# Get all available data
users_2016 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:24']  # Just user_ids
reviews_data = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']  # Reviews by 2016 users
businesses_sample = locals()['var_functions.query_db:46']  # Sample of businesses

# Extract categories more accurately
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    categories = []
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in A, B, and C"
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        text = description[start:]
        # Stop at period or end
        if '.' in text:
            text = text[:text.find('.')]
        
        # Split by comma and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',|\band\b', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part and part not in ['including', 'including', 'including', '']:
                # Remove trailing 'and' and clean up
                part = part.rstrip(',').strip()
                if part:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 2: "including A, B, C"
    elif 'including' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('including') + len('including')
        text = description[start:]
        if '.' in text:
            text = text[:text.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',|\band\b', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip()
            if part:
                categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Create business map for sample
business_map = {}
for business in businesses_sample:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    if '_' in business_id:
        business_num = business_id.split('_')[1]
        business_map[business_num] = business

# Count reviews per category in our sample
category_counts = Counter()
matched_reviews = 0

for review in reviews_data:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        if business_num in business_map:
            matched_reviews += 1
            business = business_map[business_num]
            cats = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            for cat in cats:
                category_counts[cat] += 1

# Sort categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'total_users_2016': len(users_2016),
    'total_reviews_by_2016_users': len(reviews_data),
    'matched_businesses': matched_reviews,
    'unique_categories': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5,
    'all_categories': dict(category_counts)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Read the full business data from file
business_file = "data/query_db/result_ef1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c.json"
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data (already in memory)
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping (businessid_X -> business)
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Extract categories from description
def get_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in A, B, and C."
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|including|categories of|in the categories of)\s+([^.]+)',
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',|\sand\s', text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                # Clean up the category name
                part = part.rstrip('.').strip()
                if part and len(part) < 50:  # Avoid long text
                    categories.append(part)
            break
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
business_reviews_count = {}

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_id]
            categories = get_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            
            for cat in categories:
                category_counts[cat] += 1
                
            # Also track total reviews per business for verification
            if business_id not in business_reviews_count:
                business_reviews_count[business_id] = 0
            business_reviews_count[business_id] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'total_businesses': len(businesses),
    'matched_businesses': len(business_reviews_count),
    'total_categories': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5,
    'sample_businesses': dict(list(business_reviews_count.items())[:5])
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Read the full business data from file
business_file = "data/query_db/result_ef1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c.json"
with open(business_file, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data (already in memory)
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping (businessid_X -> business)
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Extract categories from description
def get_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in A, B, and C."
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|including|categories of|in the categories of)\s+([^.]+)',
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',|\sand\s', text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                # Clean up the category name
                part = part.rstrip('.').strip()
                if part and len(part) < 50:  # Avoid long text
                    categories.append(part)
            break
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
business_reviews_count = {}

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_id]
            categories = get_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            
            for cat in categories:
                category_counts[cat] += 1
                
            # Also track total reviews per business for verification
            if business_id not in business_reviews_count:
                business_reviews_count[business_id] = 0
            business_reviews_count[business_id] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'total_businesses': len(businesses),
    'matched_businesses': len(business_reviews_count),
    'total_categories': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5,
    'sample_businesses': dict(list(business_reviews_count.items())[:5])
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Get the file path for businesses and read all data
businesses_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:50']
with open(businesses_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Extract categories from business description
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Look for patterns like:
    # "services in A, B, and C."
    # "including A, B, C, and D."
    # "in the categories of A, B, and C."
    
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|including|categories of|in the categories of)\s+([^.]+?)(?:\.|$)',
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            # Handle different separators
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                # Clean up
                part = part.rstrip('.').strip()
                # Remove leading/trailing quotes and spaces
                part = part.strip("'\" ")
                if part and len(part) < 50:  # Avoid capturing too much text
                    categories.append(part)
            break
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
matched_businesses = set()
unmatched_refs = set()

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            matched_businesses.add(business_id)
            business = business_map[business_id]
            description = business.get('description', '')
            categories = extract_categories(description)
            
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1
        else:
            unmatched_refs.add(business_ref)

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'total_businesses_available': len(businesses),
    'matched_businesses': len(matched_businesses),
    'unmatched_business_refs': len(unmatched_refs),
    'total_categories_found': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Get the file path for businesses and read all data
businesses_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:50']
with open(businesses_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Extract categories from business description
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Look for patterns like:
    # "services in A, B, and C."
    # "including A, B, C, and D."
    # "in the categories of A, B, and C."
    
    patterns = [
        r'(?:services in|including|categories of|in the categories of)\s+([^.]+?)(?:\.|$)',
    ]
    
    for pattern in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            # Handle different separators
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                # Clean up
                part = part.rstrip('.').strip()
                # Remove leading/trailing quotes and spaces
                part = part.strip("'\" ")
                if part and len(part) < 50:  # Avoid capturing too much text
                    categories.append(part)
            break
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
matched_businesses = set()
unmatched_refs = set()

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            matched_businesses.add(business_id)
            business = business_map[business_id]
            description = business.get('description', '')
            categories = extract_categories(description)
            
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1
        else:
            unmatched_refs.add(business_ref)

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'total_businesses_available': len(businesses),
    'matched_businesses': len(matched_businesses),
    'unmatched_business_refs': len(unmatched_refs),
    'total_categories_found': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Get the file path for businesses and read all data
businesses_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:50']
with open(businesses_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Extract categories from business description
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Look for patterns like: "services in A, B, and C."
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        text = description[start:]
        if '.' in text:
            text = text[:text.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',|\sand\s', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part:
                categories.append(part)
    elif 'including' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('including') + len('including')
        text = description[start:]
        if '.' in text:
            text = text[:text.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',|\sand\s', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part:
                categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
matched_businesses = set()

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = 'businessid_' + business_num
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            matched_businesses.add(business_id)
            business = business_map[business_id]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'matched_businesses': len(matched_businesses),
    'unique_categories': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}))

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Get the file path for businesses and read all data
businesses_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:50']
with open(businesses_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Extract categories from business description
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Look for patterns like: "services in A, B, and C."
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        text = description[start:]
        if '.' in text:
            text = text[:text.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',|\sand\s', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part:
                categories.append(part)
    elif 'including' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('including') + len('including')
        text = description[start:]
        if '.' in text:
            text = text[:text.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',|\sand\s', text)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part:
                categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
matched_businesses = set()

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = 'businessid_' + business_num
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            matched_businesses.add(business_id)
            business = business_map[business_id]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps({
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'matched_businesses': len(matched_businesses),
    'unique_categories': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Read the full business data
businesses_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:62']
with open(businesses_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Function to extract categories from description
def get_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in A, B, and C"
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 2: "including A, B, and C"
    elif 'including' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('including') + len('including')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 3: "categories of A, B, C"
    elif 'categories of' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('categories of') + len('categories of')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
matched_businesses = set()
unmatched_refs = set()

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            matched_businesses.add(business_id)
            business = business_map[business_id]
            description = business.get('description', '')
            categories = get_categories(description)
            
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1
        else:
            unmatched_refs.add(business_ref)

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'total_reviews_by_2016_users': len(reviews),
    'total_businesses_in_db': len(businesses),
    'matched_businesses': len(matched_businesses),
    'unmatched_business_refs': len(unmatched_refs),
    'unique_categories_found': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Read the full business data
businesses_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:62']
with open(businesses_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_id = business['business_id']
    business_map[business_id] = business

# Function to extract categories from description
def get_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in A, B, and C"
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        # Split by commas and 'and'
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 2: "including A, B, and C"
    elif 'including' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('including') + len('including')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 3: "categories of A, B, C"
    elif 'categories of' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('categories of') + len('categories of')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
matched_businesses = set()
unmatched_refs = set()

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            matched_businesses.add(business_id)
            business = business_map[business_id]
            description = business.get('description', '')
            categories = get_categories(description)
            
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1
        else:
            unmatched_refs.add(business_ref)

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

result = {
    'total_reviews_by_2016_users': len(reviews),
    'total_businesses_in_db': len(businesses),
    'matched_businesses': len(matched_businesses),
    'unmatched_business_refs': len(unmatched_refs),
    'unique_categories_found': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))

val_args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter
import re

# Read full business data from file
businesses_file_path = locals()['var_functions.query_db:66']
with open(businesses_file_path, 'r') as f:
    businesses = json.load(f)

# Get review data directly
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Create business map
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_map[business['business_id']] = business

# Extract categories
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Pattern 1: "services in A, B, and C."
    if 'services in' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('services in') + len('services in')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+|\&\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 2: "including A, B, and C."
    elif 'including' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('including') + len('including')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+|\&\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    # Pattern 3: "in the categories of A, B, C."
    elif 'categories of' in desc_lower:
        start = desc_lower.find('categories of') + len('categories of')
        remaining = description[start:]
        if '.' in remaining:
            remaining = remaining[:remaining.find('.')]
        
        parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+|\&\s+', remaining)
        for part in parts:
            part = part.strip().rstrip('.').strip()
            if part and len(part) < 50:
                categories.append(part)
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_counts = Counter()
matched_reviews = 0

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' in business_ref:
        business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
        
        if business_id in business_map:
            matched_reviews += 1
            business = business_map[business_id]
            description = business.get('description', '')
            categories = extract_categories(description)
            
            for category in categories:
                category_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_counts.most_common(5)

# Format results
result = {
    'total_reviews_by_2016_users': len(reviews),
    'matched_to_businesses': matched_reviews,
    'unique_categories': len(category_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5,
    'all_categories_count': dict(category_counts)
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
import re

# Get user IDs who registered in 2016
users_2016 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:72']
user_ids_2016 = [u['user_id'] for u in users_2016]

# Get reviews by these users
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Get all businesses 
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:66']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_map[business['business_id']] = business

# Extract categories from description
def extract_categories(description):
    """Extract business categories from description text."""
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Find the categories list in description
    # Pattern: "services in A, B, and C." or "including A, B, and C."
    patterns = [
        (r'services in\s+([^.]+)', 'services in'),
        (r'including\s+([^.]+)', 'including'),
        (r'categories of\s+([^.]+)', 'categories of'),
        (r'in the categories of\s+([^.]+)', 'in the categories of')
    ]
    
    for pattern, keyword in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Remove trailing period if any
            text = text.rstrip('.')
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', text)
            
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                # Clean up the category name
                # Remove leading/trailing quotes, spaces, etc.
                part = part.strip("'\" ")
                if part and len(part) < 100:  # Filter out very long text
                    categories.append(part)
            break  # Only process first matching pattern
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = Counter()
business_category_map = {}  # Cache categories per business
reviews_per_business = Counter()  # Track reviews per business

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' not in business_ref:
        continue
    
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
    business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
    
    reviews_per_business[business_id] += 1
    
    # Get categories for this business
    if business_id in business_category_map:
        categories = business_category_map[business_id]
    else:
        if business_id in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_id]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            business_category_map[business_id] = categories
        else:
            categories = []
            business_category_map[business_id] = categories
    
    # Count reviews for each category
    for category in categories:
        category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_counts.most_common(5)

# Also get total reviews that were matched to businesses
matched_reviews = sum(1 for review in reviews if '_' in review['business_ref'] and 
                     f'businessid_{review["business_ref"].split("_")[1]}' in business_map)

result = {
    'total_users_2016': len(user_ids_2016),
    'total_reviews_by_2016_users': len(reviews),
    'reviews_matched_to_businesses': matched_reviews,
    'unique_businesses_reviewed': len(reviews_per_business),
    'businesses_with_category_info': len([b for b, cats in business_category_map.items() if cats]),
    'total_unique_categories': len(category_review_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5_categories
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

var_functions.query_db:6.text: Sure, it's cheap, but there isn't much to see. I think you'd have to have a big interest in the topic to find it exciting, and kids would be bored. I think it only lasted maybe 10-15 minutes. Our tour person was somewhat knowledgeable and seemed into it, but he didn't give us much time to read the information on each level. I had to take photos of the plaques to read later, and half of them need replacing, as they are so sun-bleached they're virtually unreadable (tour guide said they're being replaced soon and that the other half were already replaced). I really thought it needed to be higher up to give a good view. The Lewis and Clark State Historic Site just down the road was more interesting and free. If you live around here like I do and have nothing better to do, you might want to give it a go if the topic interests you, but if you're a tourist, this is not something you should waste your time on.
Very good service but a little pricey for the services your receive. Clean and sanitary too

My friend and I enjoyed a fantastic meal at Miles Table and I can't wait to return! Given that it was half-price-burger-day, I felt the need to give the falafel burger a shot. It was delicious! I'm not always a falafel fan, but this "burger" was awesome. The brioche bun was the perfect consistency and the portion of fries was plentiful. My friend enjoyed the cobb salad (though she requested balsamic dressing instead of buttermilk and she received buttermilk). The restaurant is quaint and has ample seating. While you order at the counter, our "server" (I believe her name was Sarah) was fantastic and made sure we had everything we needed. Overall, this is a great spot and I will be back soon...I may have to make it a weekly lunch staple!

import json
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
import re

# Get user IDs who registered in 2016
users_2016 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:72']
user_ids_2016 = [u['user_id'] for u in users_2016]

# Get reviews by these users
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']

# Get all businesses 
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:66']

# Create business mapping: businessid_X -> business info
business_map = {}
for business in businesses:
    business_map[business['business_id']] = business

# Extract categories from description
def extract_categories(description):
    """Extract business categories from description text."""
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    # Find the categories list in description
    # Pattern: "services in A, B, and C." or "including A, B, and C."
    patterns = [
        (r'services in\s+([^.]+)', 'services in'),
        (r'including\s+([^.]+)', 'including'),
        (r'categories of\s+([^.]+)', 'categories of'),
        (r'in the categories of\s+([^.]+)', 'in the categories of')
    ]
    
    for pattern, keyword in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            # Remove trailing period if any
            text = text.rstrip('.')
            # Split by commas and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', text)
            
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                # Clean up the category name
                # Remove leading/trailing quotes, spaces, etc.
                part = part.strip("'\" ")
                if part and len(part) < 100:  # Filter out very long text
                    categories.append(part)
            break  # Only process first matching pattern
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = Counter()
business_category_map = {}  # Cache categories per business
reviews_per_business = Counter()  # Track reviews per business

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' not in business_ref:
        continue
    
    # Convert businessref_X to businessid_X
    business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
    business_id = f'businessid_{business_num}'
    
    reviews_per_business[business_id] += 1
    
    # Get categories for this business
    if business_id in business_category_map:
        categories = business_category_map[business_id]
    else:
        if business_id in business_map:
            business = business_map[business_id]
            categories = extract_categories(business.get('description', ''))
            business_category_map[business_id] = categories
        else:
            categories = []
            business_category_map[business_id] = categories
    
    # Count reviews for each category
    for category in categories:
        category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = category_review_counts.most_common(5)

# Also get total reviews that were matched to businesses
matched_reviews = sum(1 for review in reviews if '_' in review['business_ref'] and 
                     f'businessid_{review["business_ref"].split("_")[1]}' in business_map)

result = {
    'total_users_2016': len(user_ids_2016),
    'total_reviews_by_2016_users': len(reviews),
    'reviews_matched_to_businesses': matched_reviews,
    'unique_businesses_reviewed': len(reviews_per_business),
    'businesses_with_category_info': len([b for b, cats in business_category_map.items() if cats]),
    'total_unique_categories': len(category_review_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5_categories
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))

args.code:
import json
from collections import Counter, defaultdict
import re

# Get data from previous queries
users_2016 = locals()['var_functions.query_db:72']
reviews = locals()['var_functions.query_db:10']
businesses = locals()['var_functions.query_db:66']

# Create sets and mappings
user_ids_2016 = set(u['user_id'] for u in users_2016)
business_map = {b['business_id']: b for b in businesses}

# Extract categories from description
def extract_categories(description):
    if not description:
        return []
    
    categories = []
    desc_lower = description.lower()
    
    patterns = [
        (r'services in\s+([^.]+)', 'services in'),
        (r'including\s+([^.]+)', 'including'),
        (r'categories of\s+([^.]+)', 'categories of')
    ]
    
    for pattern, keyword in patterns:
        match = re.search(pattern, desc_lower, re.IGNORECASE)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1).rstrip('.')
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\s+and\s+', text)
            
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip("'\" ")
                if part and len(part) < 100:
                    categories.append(part)
            break
    
    return categories

# Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = Counter()
reviews_per_business = Counter()
business_category_map = {}

for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    if '_' not in business_ref:
        continue
    
    business_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_num
    
    reviews_per_business[business_id] += 1
    
    if business_id in business_category_map:
        categories = business_category_map[business_id]
    else:
        if business_id in business_map:
            categories = extract_categories(business_map[business_id].get('description', ''))
            business_category_map[business_id] = categories
        else:
            categories = []
            business_category_map[business_id] = categories
    
    for category in categories:
        category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Get top 5 categories
top_5 = category_review_counts.most_common(5)

# Count matched reviews
matched_reviews = sum(1 for r in reviews if '_' in r['business_ref'] and 'businessid_' + r['business_ref'].split('_')[1] in business_map)

result = {
    'total_users_2016': len(user_ids_2016),
    'total_reviews': len(reviews),
    'reviews_matched': matched_reviews,
    'unique_businesses': len(reviews_per_business),
    'businesses_with_categories': len([b for b, cats in business_category_map.items() if cats]),
    'unique_categories': len(category_review_counts),
    'top_5_categories': top_5
}

print('__RESULT__:')
print(json.dumps(result))
